narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, looking at their academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and more to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 35 (slightly under the 36.70 average), Year 3 school decile ranking of 1 (far below the average 5.53), Year 1 decile of 2 (versus the average 5.71), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 64.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the start. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your low Year 3 decile ranking, which signals weaker performance relative to peers in your later undergraduate years and strongly raised concerns about bar exam readiness. Next in importance, your LSAT score helped pull the prediction in a more positive direction, as it was close to average and suggested solid potential despite other hurdles. Your middle family income bracket had a smaller but still negative influence, nudging the model toward seeing higher failure risk compared to students from higher-income backgrounds who often have more resources for exam prep. Overall, your academic deciles stood out as much lower than typical successful applicants, outweighing the milder boosts from your LSAT and common full-time status. While these patterns are drawn from real student outcomes, they don't capture your full story or determination. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score further, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as a Black man pursuing law. Many students in similar positions improve their profiles and gain admission elsewhere—reach out to admissions counselors for targeted advice, and keep pushing, because your path to law school is far from over.
